title: Lesson 264
transcript: backup and storage strategies. These are different techniques that we can use to backup our data. So if we have a failure in one of our systems, one of our hard drives, we can recover our data. Now we're gonna talk about different types of backup strategies like incremental and differential. But before we explain those, we have to talk about one very important bit. It's known as the archive bit. Now in an operating system, we have some metadata, data about data for each file. You can see this in Windows if you write or in any operating system. If you right-click on a file, hit Properties, it'll list last creation date, who the file was created by, creation date. This is all metadata. One of the pieces of metadata that's stored for every single file is the archive bit. And the archive bit is simply set to either one or to zero, one binary bit, that's all it is. And if the archive bit is set to zero, it means no backup is required. Whereas if the archive bit is set to one, it means the file needs to be backed up. So keep that in mind because this is a key difference. How we treat this archive bit is a very important difference between incremental and differential backups. To explain incremental and differential backups, we have a simple little graph here. It shows you the amount of data that's backed up on this axis, and the days of the week. And a lot of backup strategies start on, say, a Sunday, so let's start on Sunday night. With an incremental backup, you start the first day of the week, typically Sunday, with a full backup. So we'd back up all the data. And what do we do with that archive bit? Well, for all these files that we've backed up, we set the archive bit to zero. Now, Monday morning rolls around. People come into work, they start modifying, creating files. For every file that is created or modified, the archive bit is set to zero. One, meaning the file needs to be backed up. Monday night rolls around. What do we back up? Well, we back up all the files that have been changed on Monday. And in an incremental backup, we reset the archive bit to zero. So for all the files that we backed up that night, we reset the archive bit to zero. Now, Tuesday morning rolls along. People come into work, they create modified files. For any new modified files, those files, archive bit is set to one. Tuesday night, we back up all the changes for Tuesday. So in incremental backups, it keeps going like this. Every single day, we just back up the changes that have occurred that day. So essentially what we're backing up is all the changes since the last incremental backup. So that's incremental. Let's look at differential, and then we'll talk about the differences between these. So here's differential, same exact graph, starts out exactly the same way. With a differential backup, you do a full backup. And when you do that full backup, you reset the archive bit to zero for all of those files. Monday morning, people come into work, start creating, modifying files, those files archive bit gets set to one. And so Monday night, you're backing up all the changes since the last full backup looks exactly like incremental so far. But here's the key difference. in a differential backup on Monday night during the first backup, you do not reset the archive bit. You leave the archive bit set to one. And so now, Tuesday rolls around. People start coming into work, modifying files, and now what do you backup Tuesday night? Well, because you didn't reset the archive bit on Monday, you're backing up all the changes that occurred on Monday and all the changes that occurred on Tuesday. So you're backing up more data. Same thing Wednesday, you're backing up all the changes that occurred on Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday. And so every night, the amount of data that you're backing up grows. So what are the pros and cons of these two different strategies? Pros of incremental. It uses less data overall. You can see you're using less data. And the nightly backup jobs for most of the week run much faster. But the big disadvantage of incremental is that if you have a data loss and let's say that you have it on say like Saturday night, how many tapes do you have to pull? Well, you have to pull the full backup and then every single incremental up to the day that you had the failure. So how many tapes would you potentially have to pull? up to seven. Whereas in differential, it's kind of the inverse. What's the maximum, what's the big advantage of differential? It's how many tapes you'd ever have to pull. And what's the maximum number of tapes you'd ever have to pull? Well, it doesn't matter when you have a failure. The maximum number of tapes you'd ever have to pull is two. the full backup and the most recent differential. So the big advantage of differential is the recovery time is the fastest, but the downsides uses a lot more data and the backups each night get longer and longer as the week goes on. So here's a summary of the different backup strategies. And by the way, a good way of summarizing differentials backups is that you backup all the changes since the last full backup. And here's that summary of the different backup strategies. Now, I haven't talked about mirroring yet, so let me talk about that one here. What's the idea of mirroring? Well, mirroring is where you make a full and complete copy of a system's data. And the important part with mirroring is that you do not compress the data. Most of the time when you're doing backups, in order to make the backups fit in as little space as possible, you're compressing the data. And that compression stage actually takes a fair bit of time. And the same thing when you read the data back off the tapes, the decompression can take a while. So in mirroring, there is no compression. It's just an exact copy, which means that the backup time, very fast, the restore time, very fast. But the amount of storage required because there's no compression, very, very high. Full backup, we already talked about. You backup all the data. And what's the difference between full backup and mirroring? Well, in a full backup, you are backing up, in both of them, you're backing up all the data. But in a full backup, you are compressing the data. So that means it's quite slow to back up all the data like that. And the restoration time's decent. And we already talked about incremental and differential. So just remember differential ads, you're backing up since the last full backup. And incremental, you're backing up since the last incremental backup. Now, a couple other pieces to talk about here. These are different sort of backup strategies. First, offsite storage. This is the idea that when you're creating backups, backup tapes for instance, you don't wanna store the backup tape right beside the primary system. Because if your primary system has some sort of failure, power outage, it burns down, you don't want your backup tape sitting right beside it. Next is this idea of electronic vaulting. Electronic vaulting is simply using an automated tape management system. It usually looks like a big silo. It's got an automated robotic arm inside. It's filled with tapes and that robotic arm can grab tapes, put them into the readers automatically. It automates the whole process of managing all of these tapes. And then finally, tape rotation. You've probably heard of different techniques for reusing backup tapes. And there's things like father and grandfather and etc. Never get questions about that on the exam though, so don't worry about all these different tape rotation strategies that are out there. I don't think you need to know them. So there you go. That's backup and restoration. you
